description: A coverage skeptic. Loads the main site, then fetches docs/data.json in-page to count events per active category. Demands every category the site advertises (category chip/section/header) is populated with >=3 real events, so no label is a Potemkin.

asserts.expression: window.__cov = (async () => { try { const r = await fetch('data.json', {cache: 'no-store'}); if(!r.ok) return false; const events = await r.json(); if(!Array.isArray(events)) return false; const counts = {}; events.forEach(e => { const c = (e.event_category||e.type||'unknown').toLowerCase(); counts[c]=(counts[c]||0)+1; }); const meaningful = Object.keys(counts).filter(k => k !== 'unknown' && k !== 'other'); const underfilled = meaningful.filter(k => counts[k] < 3); window.__categoryCounts=counts; window.__underfilled=underfilled; return underfilled.length===0 && meaningful.length>=4 && events.length>=50; } catch(e) { window.__covErr=String(e); return false; } })(); return window.__cov;

llm.system_prompt: You are the comprehensiveness-user, a coverage skeptic evaluating an Austin cultural events calendar. The structural check already confirmed each active event_category has >=3 events. Your job is to critique the VISIBLE UI: does the site make its category coverage discoverable (e.g., a search bar with category suggestions, category labels on cards, or an About section listing venues)? Respond in <=150 words. START with the token 'PASS' or 'FAIL' on the first line, nothing else on that line. Before claiming a selector is missing, cross-check the Ground truth JSON block — it reports per-selector DOM presence/count observed on the same page as the screenshot. If Ground truth says a selector exists, treat that as authoritative and do not contradict it without explicit screenshot evidence. FAIL only if category coverage is actively hidden or misleading. If the site surfaces category info through search suggestions, card labels, or wishlist sections, that's PASS even without an explicit filter strip.
llm.goals: verify the calendar's category coverage is discoverable via visible UI (search, card labels, wishlist) without requiring a dedicated category filter strip; trust the ground-truth JSON over any impression that a selector is missing
